body: A punishing weather double-hit: strong thunderstorm winds caused widespread power outages across a broad swath of the Midwest, Northeast and Ontario, even as the region was already dealing with a heat wave. The available report is brief, but the scale is the story — about 1 million customers without power in conditions where cooling is not a luxury.
dek: Thunderstorm winds cut electricity to roughly 1 million customers across the Midwest, Northeast and Ontario.
headline: Storms knock out power as heat bears down
why_it_matters: Outages during heat waves are more dangerous than routine storm damage: they turn grid fragility into an immediate public-health problem, especially for older residents, medically vulnerable people and anyone without backup cooling.

body: Trump’s latest clemency moves are being framed not as mercy at the margins, but as policy by pardon. According to the report, he pardoned people who violated the Clean Air Act, further weakening environmental law enforcement, and also helped a political supporter who had pleaded guilty in a fraud involving Jack Abramoff. With only one excerpt available, the details of each case are limited, but the editorial thrust is clear: clemency is being used to protect allies and signal hostility to regulation.
dek: The president pardoned Clean Air Act violators and a donor who pleaded guilty in a fraud case tied to Jack Abramoff.
headline: Trump uses pardons to undercut environmental enforcement
why_it_matters: Presidential pardons are legally broad but politically revealing. When they touch environmental crimes and donor-linked fraud, they can chill enforcement and tell regulated industries which violations may no longer carry real consequences.

body: A sewage spill into the Hudson River continued for hours after a power failure at a treatment plant just north of New York City. The discharge ran from Thursday night to Friday afternoon, according to the report. Officials did not expect it to affect New York City’s Fourth of July celebrations — a narrow reassurance that still leaves the larger environmental and infrastructure questions hanging.
dek: A power failure at a plant north of New York City sent sewage into the river from Thursday night into Friday afternoon.
headline: Sewage spill hits the Hudson after treatment-plant failure
why_it_matters: Wastewater systems are invisible until they fail. A power-related spill underscores how basic urban infrastructure depends on redundancy, maintenance and fast response — especially around heavily used waterways.

body: Simon Willison flags a useful piece of infrastructure for people trying to understand “open source AI” as something more concrete than a slogan. Current AI — described as a global partnership building a public option for AI, founded as a nonprofit at the Paris AI Action Summit in February 2025 and backed by $400 million in committed capital — has launched Gap Map v0.1. The map details 421 products: 266 software tools and libraries, 85 models, 50 datasets and 20 hardware projects, produced by 228 organizations. They are organized into 14 categories across three layers of the stack: model components, product/UX and infrastructure. The excerpt also notes a much larger universe: 24,400 uncategorized artifacts in the long tail.
dek: Current AI’s Gap Map v0.1 catalogs hundreds of tools, models, datasets and hardware projects — and hints at a much larger uncategorized long tail.
headline: A first map of the open-source AI stack arrives
why_it_matters: Open-source AI is often debated at the level of ideology. A map like this makes the ecosystem inspectable: what exists, where the gaps are, and which parts of the stack are actually shared infrastructure rather than marketing language.

body: The AI Engineer World’s Fair appears to have ended less with victory laps than with a practical argument about the shape of the work ahead. Latent Space’s dispatch says the close featured a debate about loops, a report on the state of AI engineering, and keynotes focused on what builders should make next. The excerpt does not define the “loops” dispute, so the takeaway has to stay broad: the event is capturing a field moving from model awe toward engineering patterns, product choices and repeatable systems.
dek: The AI Engineer World’s Fair wrapped with a “great loops” debate, a state-of-the-field report and closing keynotes about what to build next.
headline: AI engineers end their fair debating loops — and what comes next
why_it_matters: AI engineering is becoming its own discipline, with its own arguments about architecture and workflow. What practitioners normalize now — loops, agents, evaluation, infrastructure — will shape the next wave of AI products more than splashy demos alone.

body: After more than 20 years living in Jersey City while building recognized Indian restaurants in Manhattan, restaurateur Jimmy Rizvi is finally opening for the community at home. Eater reports that Rizvi — behind Gramercy’s GupShup and the popular East Village restaurant Bungalow with Vikas Khanna of MasterChef India — had long watched Jersey City’s large South Asian community. The duplicated source listing adds no new detail, but the headline’s claim is pointed: this is being pitched as the Indian restaurant Jersey City has been waiting for.
dek: Jimmy Rizvi, known for GupShup and Bungalow, is turning attention to the South Asian community in his own backyard.
headline: Jersey City gets a long-awaited Indian restaurant from a Manhattan power player
why_it_matters: Restaurant openings can be civic signals. Here, the story is not just another expansion from Manhattan outward; it is about a restaurateur serving a large diaspora community where he has lived for decades.

body: RogerEbert.com’s latest “Female Filmmakers in Focus” entry turns to Alice Winocour and “Couture,” described in the excerpt as an emotive cancer drama starring Angelina Jolie. The two source entries appear to be the same interview surfaced through different feeds, so there is no competing account here — just a pointer to a filmmaker conversation centered on authorship, performance and illness in a high-profile drama.
dek: RogerEbert.com’s “Female Filmmakers in Focus” interviews the director behind “Couture.”
headline: Alice Winocour discusses her Angelina Jolie cancer drama
why_it_matters: Interviews like this can add texture beyond the review cycle, especially for films whose subject matter risks being reduced to prestige shorthand. Hearing from the director helps clarify intent, craft and emotional framing.

body: Angela Nikolau and Ivan Beerkus, extreme climbers known for illegally scaling buildings around the world, were arrested after getting engaged atop the Empire State Building. Prosecutors accuse them of breaking a security door to reach the spire of the Manhattan landmark. The excerpt gives only the allegation and arraignment context, so the legal picture remains preliminary — but the episode sits squarely in the modern genre of daredevil spectacle meeting urban security reality.
dek: Extreme climbers Angela Nikolau and Ivan Beerkus were arrested after allegedly breaking a security door to reach the spire.
headline: Empire State Building engagement stunt ends in criminal allegations
why_it_matters: Iconic buildings are both symbols and targets for attention-seeking stunts. Cases like this test how cities balance public fascination, private security, trespass law and the risk that viral spectacle encourages copycats.
